examples.title: Process Creation Using fork()
examples.description: Demonstrates how a new process is created in Unix/Linux using fork() system call
examples.code:
BEGIN
    PRINT "Parent process started, PID = ", getpid()
    
    pid = fork()  // Create new process
    
    IF pid == -1 THEN
        PRINT "Fork failed"
        EXIT
    ELSE IF pid == 0 THEN
        // This is the child process
        PRINT "Child process started, PID = ", getpid()
        PRINT "Parent PID = ", getppid()
        
        // Execute new program
        execve("/bin/ls", args, env)
        
        PRINT "This won't print if exec succeeds"
        EXIT
    ELSE
        // This is the parent process
        PRINT "Parent: Child PID = ", pid
        
        // Wait for child to complete
        wait(NULL)
        PRINT "Child process completed"
    END IF
    
    PRINT "Process exiting"
END
examples.explanation: The fork() system call creates an exact copy of the parent process. The child gets a copy of the parent's address space. After fork(), both parent and child continue execution. The parent calls wait() to wait for child completion. The child can use execve() to replace its memory with a new program.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is size of address space copied

examples.title: Process Scheduling Simulation
examples.description: Simple demonstration of process scheduling with different states
examples.code:
from collections import deque
import time

class Process:
    def __init__(self, pid, name, burst_time, priority):
        self.pid = pid
        self.name = name
        self.burst_time = burst_time
        self.priority = priority
        self.remaining_time = burst_time
        self.state = "NEW"  # NEW, READY, RUNNING, WAITING, TERMINATED

class Scheduler:
    def __init__(self):
        self.ready_queue = deque()
        self.waiting_queue = deque()
        self.current_process = None
    
    def add_process(self, process):
        process.state = "READY"
        self.ready_queue.append(process)
        print(f"Process {process.name} (PID {process.pid}) added to ready queue")
    
    def simulate_context_switch(self):
        if self.current_process:
            self.current_process.state = "READY"
            self.ready_queue.append(self.current_process)
            print(f"Context switch: {self.current_process.name} -> READY")
        
        if self.ready_queue:
            self.current_process = self.ready_queue.popleft()
            self.current_process.state = "RUNNING"
            print(f"Context switch: {self.current_process.name} -> RUNNING")

# Example usage
scheduler = Scheduler()
p1 = Process(1, "Process1", 10, 1)
p2 = Process(2, "Process2", 5, 2)
p3 = Process(3, "Process3", 8, 1)

scheduler.add_process(p1)
scheduler.add_process(p2)
scheduler.add_process(p3)

print("\n--- Scheduling Simulation ---")
scheduler.simulate_context_switch()
scheduler.current_process.remaining_time -= 2
scheduler.simulate_context_switch()
examples.explanation: This code simulates basic process scheduling. It shows how processes move between different states (NEW, READY, RUNNING). The scheduler maintains ready queue and performs context switching by moving processes between states. Demonstrates process management concepts like PCB simulation and scheduling.

examples.title: Context Switching Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates how the OS performs context switching between two processes
examples.code:
BEGIN
    // Process A is currently running, Process B is in ready queue
    
    PROCEDURE context_switch(from_process, to_process):
        
        // Step 1: Save context of currently running process
        from_process.pcb.program_counter = PC
        from_process.pcb.cpu_registers = CURRENT_REGISTERS
        from_process.pcb.stack_pointer = SP
        from_process.pcb.cpu_status = CPU_FLAGS
        from_process.pcb.state = "READY"
        
        // Step 2: Update process control blocks
        from_process.pcb.cpu_time = from_process.pcb.cpu_time + time_slice
        
        // Step 3: Save current process in ready queue
        ADD_TO_READY_QUEUE(from_process)
        
        // Step 4: Remove next process from ready queue
        to_process = REMOVE_FROM_READY_QUEUE()
        to_process.state = "RUNNING"
        
        // Step 5: Restore context of new process
        PC = to_process.pcb.program_counter
        CURRENT_REGISTERS = to_process.pcb.cpu_registers
        SP = to_process.pcb.stack_pointer
        CPU_FLAGS = to_process.pcb.cpu_status
        
        // Step 6: Load memory management info
        LOAD_MEMORY_MANAGER(to_process.pcb.page_table)
        
        // Step 7: Jump to new process execution
        JUMP_TO(PC)
        
        RETURN
    
    // Timer interrupt triggers context switch
    IF timer_interrupt THEN
        context_switch(current_process, next_ready_process)
    END IF
END
examples.explanation: Context switching involves saving the complete state of the running process (registers, PC, stack pointer) into its PCB, then loading the state of another ready process. The PCB acts as a snapshot that allows processes to be suspended and resumed exactly from where they left off.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) - context switch is constant time operation
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) - only saves/restores fixed number of registers
